---
format: revealjs
---

# Today's Agenda {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" }

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
```

<br>

::: {.r-fit-text}
**Paper 2: Review Outline and Start Writing**
:::

<br>

::: r-stack
Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
:::

::: notes
Prep for Class

1. Today class needs to decide if next week we meet in computer lab
    - SP24: Sonja has reserved Lay 208 if we want it
    - If class chooses the lab then prep an email reminder for Monday
    
<br>

**SLIDE**: Prompt for deciding if computer lab needed
:::



## Next Week: Fusion Posters {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

**Getting Feedback from our Community**

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/11_1-FD22 wide shot.jpg")
```

::: notes
Next week we take your material from the second paper and use it to prepare a Fusion Day poster.

<br>

I have reserved a computer lab for us for next week (Lay 208)

**Do we want to work here in our classroom on your laptops or use the lab?**
:::



## Paper 2 {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

<br>

Which of the four policy design options is the "right" choice for addressing your environmental problem?

- Analyze the pros and cons of each option for your chosen problem

::: notes
**Questions on the prompt?**
:::



## For Today {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

<br>

::: {.r-fit-text}
Convert your notes into an outline

- Policy examples with pros and cons lists

- Add real-world examples
:::

::: notes
Last class we brainstormed specific executions of each policy design option

- We then used those options to gather feedback from your classmates

<br>

My hope is that this exercise helped to clarify in your mind how each policy design can be applied to your specific case 

<br>

**Does everybody have an outline to share?**

<br>

15 mins: Ok, pair off and get/give feedback on the outlines

<br>

**Alright, how are we doing?**

- **Any questions I can help with?**

<br>

Before we transition from the outline to the paper I want to highlight a few important elements

- This paper represents your first effort to make a policy argument

- This means you are now actively pushing an argument and not just describing a problem

- **SLIDE**: Let's talk about how this changes things...
:::



## Paper 2 as a Policy Argument {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

<br>

**1. The introduction must be compelling**

- What problem are you working to solve?

- Why should they care? 

- What is your argument?

::: notes
The target of a policy argument is almost certainly a stakeholder in your community

- This could be a community group who agrees we have a problem, 

- someone who doesn't realize they are impacted by the problem, OR

- An actor whose behavior you are trying to change!

<br>

In any case, you need their attention if you have any hope of getting them to join your "team"

<br>

This means your intro has to accomplish three tasks:

- Make them understand what you are concerned about,

- Make them want to read it,

- AND, they should know exactly what your answer is to the assignment question by the end of the intro

<br>

**Questions?**
:::



## Paper 2 as a Policy Argument {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

<br>

**2. Don't assume the reader is an expert in the problem OR the policy options**

- Clearly define all concepts (with citations)

- Provide real-world examples


::: notes
Ok, the intro is compelling and you've got them into the body of the paper

- Now you have to keep their attention!

- People are busy, they don't want to feel stupid and they aren't going to struggle through a poorly written or unclear argument

- This means you HAVE to write to keep them moving through your argument

<br>

Don't assume the reader is an expert in the problem you are working on, their role in that problem or how any of the design approaches work.

- Your job is to create a shared baseline in your problem and the design options

- Make sure you and they are on the same page

<br>

So, your argument must be built on clear definitions, cited from good sources and illustrated by examples

<br>

**Questions?**
:::



## Paper 2 as a Policy Argument {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

<br>

**3. Support ALL claims with evidence**

- X is happening in our community...

- X is harming our community...

- Policy X is effective when...

- Policy X is ineffective when...

::: notes
Ok, their in the weeds now and now is when you need to start selling them your analysis

- The bigger the "ask", the harder to sell

- A tough sell that relies on your opinion will go nowhere, hence a policy ARGUMENT not a policy op-ed

<br>

Key to this paper is how you evaluate each policy design when it is applied to your problem

- Without evidence, these claims would just be your opinion

<br>

**Questions?**
:::



## Paper 2 as a Policy Argument {background-image="libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png" .center}

<br>

**Which of the four policy design options is the "right" choice for addressing your environmental problem?**

- Draw the reader in

- Don't assume they are an expert

- Support ALL claims with evidence

::: notes
**Questions on any of these elements?**

<br>

Let's get to writing!

- And don't forget that next week we work on the Fusion Posters in class
:::
